ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers shawarma as a food product category; no lamb-specific shawarma term exists, so closest_parent is used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers sheep/lamb meat products; no specific shawarma preparation code exists in FoodEx2, so closest_parent sheep meat products is used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for lamb shawarma exists; SNOMED CT does not provide granular prepared-dish codes at this level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard lamb shawarma wrap as typically served in a Singapore Turkish restaurant, comprising approximately 280 g of marinated rotisserie lamb, flatbread, vegetables, and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb shawarma (composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central: Lamb, composite of retail cuts, cooked, lean and fat; Pita bread, white; Tahini; and standard shawarma condiment components)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for lamb shawarma as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked lamb (NDB 17003 / FDC 174351), pita bread white (FDC 168012), tahini (FDC 168604), tomato, onion, and parsley. Proportions based on a standard 280 g wrap: approximately 120 g cooked lamb, 80 g pita, 30 g tahini sauce, 50 g vegetables. Per-100 g values derived by weighted average of components. This is a medium-confidence estimate; a direct laboratory analysis or HPB composite entry would be preferred.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed lamb and flatbread wrap
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for lamb shawarma exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate derived from: white pita bread GI ~57 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021), moderated by the substantial protein and fat content from lamb and tahini which slow gastric emptying and reduce the glycemic response. Mixed meat-and-bread wraps with similar macronutrient profiles (protein ~13 g/100 g, fat ~10 g/100 g) are consistently reported in the low-to-medium GI range (GI 40–55) in peer-reviewed literature. GI 45 (low category) is a conservative defensible estimate. GL per serving = (45 × 18.5 × 2.80) / 100 = 23.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1176 mg per 280 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single lamb shawarma wrap provides approximately 1176 mg sodium (~51% of the 2300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as pickles or extra tahini.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from flatbread (~18.5 g carbohydrate/100 g; ~52 g per serving) with low GI (~45) attenuated by high protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low glycemic index (~45) and high protein content of lamb shawarma produce a moderate glycemic load (~23 per serving), making occasional consumption acceptable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled and paired with non-starchy vegetables; the flatbread component should be monitored as part of total daily carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.4 g/100 g; ~9.5 g per serving) and sodium from lamb and marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb shawarma contains moderate saturated fat (~9.5 g per serving) and high sodium (~1176 mg per serving), both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD should limit frequency of consumption, choose leaner cuts, and request reduced-salt preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb shawarma is a popular Middle Eastern and Turkish street food consisting of thinly sliced marinated lamb that has been slow-roasted on a vertical rotating spit (rotisserie) and then shaved off and served wrapped in flatbread such as pita or lavash. The lamb is typically marinated for several hours in a spiced yoghurt mixture containing cumin, turmeric, coriander, paprika, cinnamon, garlic, and lemon juice, which tenderises the meat and imparts a deeply aromatic, slightly smoky flavour. The wrap is assembled with the sliced lamb, fresh tomato, onion, parsley, and a generous drizzle of tahini or garlic sauce. The texture is a satisfying combination of juicy, tender meat with soft flatbread and crisp fresh vegetables. In Singapore, lamb shawarma is served at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants and is popular as a hearty lunch or dinner option. Nutritionally, a standard 280 g serving provides approximately 610 kcal, 38 g protein, 29 g fat, and 52 g carbohydrate. The protein content is high due to the lamb, making it satiating. Sodium is elevated (~1176 mg per serving) due to the marinade and sauces, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), moderated by the high protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying. For people with Type 2 Diabetes, the moderate glycemic load (~23 per serving) means portion control and carbohydrate monitoring remain important. The dish is halal-certified when prepared with halal lamb.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lamb shawarma wrap?
What is the glycemic index of shawarma?
How much protein is in lamb shawarma?
Is lamb shawarma high in sodium?
What spices are used in lamb shawarma?
Difference between shawarma and doner kebab

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA) provides a direct composite entry for lamb shawarma as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked lamb, pita bread, tahini, and vegetables using standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on pita bread GI and macronutrient moderation principles from peer-reviewed literature. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimate. All other -1 fields have been replaced with estimated values where component data was available. Nutrient values are composite estimates; a direct laboratory analysis or HPB composite dish entry would upgrade confidence to high. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for lamb shawarma was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Sodium value reflects typical restaurant preparation including marinade and sauces; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. The dish is served at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine in Singapore (field survey source). Turkish cuisine multilingual naming applied: Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are provided following Singaporean multilingual naming rules as this dish is served in Singapore. Aliases_by_language and flat aliases are fully consistent.
